channel.title: Lucas Weis Polesello | SRE & Sr Software Engineer Blog
channel.description: Lucas Weis Polesello | SRE & Sr Software Engineer Blog

item.title: Bitmaps for Access Control: Scaling Notification Fan-Out with Redis ACL Checks
item.description: Why bitmaps are an efficient way to do access-control checks for high-volume real-time notifications, and when to use them.
item.content:encoded:
Most engineers spend most of their time with familiar data structures: arrays, queues, sets, and maps.

That makes sense. They solve everyday problems well.

Bitmaps are different. They feel lower-level, more specialized, and easy to ignore.

That is a mistake, because in the right problem they are extremely effective.

This post is about one of those problems: using bitmaps to route real-time notifications without hitting the database on every message.

The problem​

In many SaaS systems, access control is built on many-to-many relationships.

Imagine something Slack-like with one more layer of permissioning.

You have:

  • users U
  • groups G
  • teams T

And the rules look like this:

  • a user belongs to one or more groups
  • a group can access one or more teams
  • a message sent to a team should only reach users who are allowed to see that team

That creates a routing problem.

Every notification must reach only the correct online recipients, even when multiple permission paths exist.

When the obvious solution stops being cheap​

For a long time, normal database queries may be enough. With the right indexes, that can work well.

The more interesting case is what happens when the system starts handling tens of thousands of notifications per second.

At that point, asking the database the same access-control question over and over gets expensive. Even if each query is acceptable on its own, the total cost starts to show up in latency, CPU, memory pressure, and infrastructure size.

That is where a different representation helps.

How to know if you have this problem​

Consider bitmaps when:

  • the same access query runs on every notification
  • the query result changes infrequently compared to how often it is asked
  • user ids can be mapped to dense numeric indexes
  • you need sub-millisecond routing decisions

Why bitmaps fit this problem​

A bitmap is compact and optimized for binary state.

That makes it a good fit for questions like:

  • does this user have access to this team?
  • is this user online right now?
  • which users satisfy both conditions?

These are yes-or-no questions, and that is where bitmaps shine.

The design​

For each tenant, you can represent both access and presence with Redis bitmaps:

teams:<tenantId>:<teamId>
online-users:<tenantId>

Each bit position maps to a stable numeric user index. In some systems that can be the raw user id. In others it is a separate dense mapping.

  • in teams:<tenantId>:<teamId>, bit 1 means that user can access the team
  • in online-users:<tenantId>, bit 1 means that user is currently online

This assumes the relationship has already been denormalized into a read-optimized format.

Instead of querying relational state for every notification, you can move the hot routing step into Redis.

Example​

A relational version might look like this:

SELECT
  ug.user
FROM
  groups ug
WHERE
  ug.teams IN (<list of teams>)
  AND ug.tenantId = <tenantId>

With bitmaps, the same routing step can be done with bitmap operations.

Here is a simplified Lua example:

local tenantId = ARGV[1]
local tmp_or_key = 'tmp:teams:' .. tenantId
local notify_key = 'tmp:notification:' .. tenantId
local online_key = 'online-users:' .. tenantId

redis.call('BITOP', 'OR', tmp_or_key, unpack(KEYS))
redis.call('BITOP', 'AND', notify_key, tmp_or_key, online_key)

local max_index = redis.call('STRLEN', notify_key) * 8
local notified_users = {}
for i = 0, max_index - 1 do
  local bit = redis.call('GETBIT', notify_key, i)
  if bit == 1 then
    table.insert(notified_users, i)
  end
end

redis.call('DEL', tmp_or_key)
redis.call('DEL', notify_key)

return notified_users

This script is meant to explain the idea, not to claim that bit-by-bit scanning is always the best extraction strategy for large bitmaps.

What happens here is simple:

  1. combine all target team bitmaps with OR
  2. intersect that result with the online-users bitmap using AND
  3. the remaining set bits are users who both have access and are online

So the routing decision becomes a few in-memory bitmap operations instead of another database query on the hot path.

Why this can be faster​

Before this kind of optimization, every notification could trigger another database read, with all the usual overhead: parsing, planning, execution, buffer checks, and sometimes disk access.

Under sustained fan-out load, that gets expensive.

With bitmaps, the hot path becomes a set of in-memory operations on compact structures. That usually means:

  • lower latency
  • more predictable routing cost
  • less repetitive read pressure on the database
  • more room for the database to focus on writes and less cacheable queries

Trade-offs​

This approach is not free.

You need:

  • dense numeric identifiers, or at least a stable mapping to bitmap positions
  • update flows that keep access and online-state bitmaps current
  • enough confidence that the read amplification you are avoiding is worth the write-side bookkeeping

And if cardinality grows far enough, it is worth studying Roaring Bitmaps as a more space-efficient representation.

Practical sizing​

If you have 100,000 users, a single bitmap is about 12.5 KB. If you have 1,000 teams, that is roughly 12.5 MB of bitmap data per tenant. Redis handles that easily. But if you have millions of users and tens of thousands of teams, Roaring Bitmaps become worth evaluating.

Takeaways​

  1. Could you do something similar with normal Sets? Yes, but memory footprint and operation cost often look worse.
  2. Bitmaps are great when the problem reduces to yes-or-no membership checks.
  3. They are especially useful when the same access-control question is asked at very high frequency.
  4. Redis can be an efficient routing engine when you model the state in a form it can process cheaply.

Bitmaps are not a general-purpose answer to every permission problem.

But for ACL-heavy notification fan-out, they can be a very sharp tool.

Quick evaluation​

Before adopting bitmaps for ACL, verify:

  • Your user ids can be mapped to dense numeric indexes
  • Team membership changes are infrequent compared to notification volume
  • You have a way to keep online-state bitmaps current
  • Your total bitmap size per tenant fits comfortably in memory
item.author: lucas.polesello@lwpsoftwares.com (Lucas Weis Polesello)

item.title: The Tale of How We Paid Back Years of Tech Debt in a Month
item.description: How an oversized IAM session object caused cascading infrastructure problems, and why trimming hot-path data models often beats scaling.
item.content:encoded:
Back in 2022 - while I was at Luma Health - we went through a major outage that cost hours of downtime, a lot of engineering effort, and a fair amount of customer frustration.

One painful discovery from that period was this: we had modeled an internal IAM session object very poorly.

That mistake stayed quiet for a long time. As the company grew, it stopped being quiet.

This post is about how a single data-modeling decision created years of infrastructure debt, and why fixing it took a month but paid for itself immediately.

Why this mattered​

In healthcare software, access-control state is core system data. Systems that handle Patient Health Information need to enforce those boundaries consistently across backend flows.

Our session object carried the data needed for that enforcement: token, settings, groups, facilities, and other metadata required to authorize work.

The problem was not the existence of the session. The problem was its size.

The modeling mistake​

At some point, we chose the convenient path: load full groups and facilities data into the session JSON and cache it.

That worked until larger customers arrived with more complex account structures.

Eventually we had session payloads reaching 2.6 MB.

That is not a small piece of authorization context anymore.

In several job-processing paths, services fetched the cached session repeatedly as work moved through the platform. Sometimes that happened dozens of times for the same logical flow. Our system averaged around 2,000 jobs per second and could spike much higher.

At that point, the issue was no longer just wasteful serialization. It had become steady network pressure between services and the cache layer.

What the impact looked like​

That oversized payload showed up everywhere:

  • unstable and slower HTTP APIs

  • oversized cache infrastructure
  • higher service-to-cache bandwidth consumption
  • pressure that forced architectural band-aids into the system

We even introduced supporting components and patterns just to reduce the blast radius:

  • a dedicated pubsub-service to offload slow event-loops/slow publishes
  • a jobless pattern where only job identifiers were published and the body was fetched elsewhere

Those were symptoms of the same underlying problem: too much data moving through the hot path.

How to spot this in your own system​

If you suspect a similar problem:

  1. Check the size of your most frequently cached objects.
  2. Multiply that size by how many times they are fetched per request.
  3. Multiply again by your request rate.
  4. If the result is in the gigabits per second range, your object model is likely too heavy for the hot path.
  5. Have Bandwidth alarms for Redis. Usually, it should not go over 70%.

The fix​

The actual code change was small compared to the impact.

Instead of loading full group documents while building the session, we introduced a feature-flagged path that selected only the fields needed in the hot path. In simplified form, the idea looked like this:

Query.select('_id')

That snippet is only illustrative. The important part is that we stopped materializing large relationship payloads into the session when the hot path only needed compact identifiers.

That was enough to build a much smaller session object without changing the higher-level authorization model.

Of course, the hard part was not writing the line. The hard part was rolling it out safely.

Why rollout took time​

Session shape was deeply embedded across many services and internal libraries, so we had to treat this as a compatibility project rather than a simple refactor.

That meant:

  • auditing service and library versions
  • searching the codebase for assumptions about session shape
  • using feature flags per tenant
  • monitoring product and platform metrics during rollout

The questions were practical and unsettling:

  • would messages stop being sent?
  • would notifications silently disappear?
  • would some customer workflows degrade in ways we would only discover later?

So we rolled it carefully.

Outcome​

The rollout took nearly a month across around 90 backend services, followed by tenant-by-tenant enablement.

The results justified the caution:

  • roughly 60% reduction in the relevant cache-related network traffic
  • much more stable API latencies
  • clear room to downsize parts of the REST layer
  • near-elimination of the network pressure created by our temporary band-aids

Just as important, we were finally able to start removing workarounds that should never have become architecture in the first place.

The deeper lesson​

The expensive part of technical debt is often not the original bug. It is the ecosystem of compensating decisions that grows around it.

One oversized payload led to:

  • bigger infrastructure
  • extra services
  • more complicated publish paths
  • more operational anxiety

All of that because a hot-path object carried far more data than it needed.

When to consider this kind of optimization​

Not every large cached object needs fixing. Consider trimming hot-path payloads when:

  • the object is fetched multiple times per workflow
  • the fetch rate scales with traffic volume
  • the object contains data that is only needed in a small fraction of use cases
  • you have already scaled infrastructure and the problem persists

Takeaway​

There is a lot of justified attention on product features, growth, and customer-facing delivery.

But backend modeling decisions matter just as much when the system starts to scale.

Sometimes the best optimization is not a fancy algorithm. It is revisiting a data model that quietly became too expensive for the path it lives on.

And sometimes one month of careful cleanup pays back years of architectural interest.
item.author: lucas.polesello@lwpsoftwares.com (Lucas Weis Polesello)

item.title: Open Source Tales #1: WebSocket Operator
item.description: How I turned an old scaling problem into an open source experiment, and why platform-native routing beats application-level workarounds for stateful systems.
item.content:encoded:
One of the most interesting problems I have worked on was something that is easy in stateless systems and much less pleasant in stateful ones: scaling WebSocket servers horizontally.

If your application keeps connection state in memory, adding more replicas is not enough. You also need a way to route a message to the replica that owns the target connection.

That was the real problem.

This post is about why application-level messaging workarounds eventually fail, and what a platform-native alternative looks like.

The constraints​

Our original environment came with a long list of restrictions:

  • we had to respect an internal framework built around model events
  • IAM filtering had to be preserved
  • Socket.IO was already part of the stack
  • RabbitMQ-based Socket.IO plugins were considered too costly
  • Redis plugins were not a good fit for our case
  • multi-tab support was required
  • we wanted to avoid adding new infrastructure if possible

In other words, this was not a clean-sheet design.

Our WebSocket servers also ran on older Socket.IO versions and barely used the framework's stronger features. At that point, they could almost have been plain WebSocket servers.

The first solution​

To scale horizontally, we used Redis Pub/Sub so WebSocket servers could forward messages to each other.

That solved the immediate routing problem, but it also made me realize something important: scaling a stateful WebSocket layer is itself a costly architectural choice.

Once you go down that path, you inherit a set of distributed-systems problems whether you wanted them or not.

The uncomfortable lessons​

The pain points were familiar:

  • observability became harder
  • Redis Pub/Sub introduced delivery and bandwidth concerns
  • connection distribution across replicas was weak
  • some replicas ran hot while others stayed cold

At some point I started thinking the real question was not "how do I scale this with Redis?" but "what if the platform should help route and rebalance connections?"

Kubernetes, proxies, sidecars, and service discovery started to look more interesting than another application-level messaging layer.

The project idea​

A couple of years later I found this article, which described a Kubernetes-based approach to distributed WebSocket routing.

That article was the spark. I had already solved a similar problem under stricter constraints, and they had solved it in a cleaner infrastructure-aware way. That made me wonder how many teams were independently reinventing roughly the same architecture.

That curiosity became websocket-operator.

What the operator does​

The project is a blueprint for running horizontally scalable WebSocket workloads on Kubernetes without depending on the older Redis Pub/Sub shape of the solution I had used before.

The central idea is simple: route connections and messages through Kubernetes-aware components instead of pushing the whole problem into a broker plugin.

The operator currently revolves around three components.

Load balancer​

This is the user-facing entrypoint.

It accepts connections and routes them toward proxy sidecars using the same connection-placement logic shared elsewhere in the system.

Proxy sidecar​

The sidecar intercepts WebSocket traffic and can proxy a message to another sidecar that actually owns the destination connection.

This is what lets the system route stateful traffic across replicas.

Controller​

The controller injects sidecars into workloads marked with the ws.operator/enabled label and manages the Kubernetes-side control loop around that setup.

The longer-term goal is to help with behaviors such as rebalancing, but that part is still project direction, not a claim that every piece is already production-complete.

Why this interested me​

This project let me combine two things I wanted at the same time:

  • deeper Go and Kubernetes operator work
  • a chance to revisit an old distributed-systems problem without the original constraints

That made it more than a coding exercise. It was a way to pressure-test a better architectural direction.

Roadmap​

The roadmap is still intentionally practical:

  • pluggable hashing strategies for connection routing
  • pluggable message-routing logic
  • support for broadcast-style delivery

The first versions assume JSON messages, but there is no good reason to stop there. Real systems may want binary payloads, text frames, or custom protocols.

Should you scale your WebSocket layer?​

Before building anything like this, ask yourself:

  • Is connection count actually the bottleneck, or is it business logic?
  • Can you vertically scale your current instances instead?
  • Is your WebSocket traffic evenly distributed, or bursty?
  • Do you have a Kubernetes-native platform to lean on?

If your answers suggest that horizontal scaling is truly necessary, then an operator-based approach is worth exploring. If not, you may be solving the wrong problem.

Takeaway​

Stateful systems become expensive in ways stateless systems do not. Before scaling a WebSocket architecture, make sure the product really needs it.

If it does, lean on the platform where possible instead of pushing every routing problem into application code.

Sometimes the right lesson from a past system is not how to rebuild it. Sometimes it is how to avoid rebuilding the same constraints.
item.author: lucas.polesello@lwpsoftwares.com (Lucas Weis Polesello)

item.title: Redis is More Than a Cache - Data Reference
item.description: Why Redis should store coordination data, not full payloads, and how we redesigned our delayed-job system around that principle.
item.content:encoded:
A few weeks after fixing our delayed-job processor, one queue started behaving badly over the weekend.

Monitoring caught it. At first it looked recoverable. By Tuesday it was clear it was not. A newly onboarded large customer was affected, which made the issue impossible to ignore.

I got pulled into the investigation early because I had worked on that part of the backend before.

The incident pointed to a deeper design flaw: we were using Redis as a payload store, not as a coordination layer. This post is about why that distinction matters and how we fixed it.

The incident​

After a few hours, we found the root cause.

Our long-polling mechanism had entered a hot loop on one queue. Jobs were being retried and delayed over and over, so that queue never drained. Because the draining process was still too coupled to the rest of the system, one pathological queue slowed down others too.

We shipped a short-term fix quickly: stop retrying after N attempts and break the loop.

That solved the incident.

It did not solve the design.

The real problem​

The original delayed-job storage put the full serialized job directly into a Redis sorted set.

That was convenient, but it created two serious problems:

  1. Redis memory usage scaled with payload size. Large jobs meant large sorted sets.

  2. A single backlog could get very expensive. We were using a large ElastiCache instance mainly to absorb spikes and pathological cases.

At the time, our delayed-job footprint had grown into the hundreds of gigabytes. That is a very expensive way to keep postponed work around.

The root cause was the same in both cases: Redis was holding full application data when it should have been holding only enough information to coordinate re-enqueueing.

How to know if this applies to you​

Look at your Redis memory usage. If your delayed-job sorted sets consume a large share of total memory, or if your Redis instance size is driven by job payload size rather than queue count, you are likely storing too much application data in Redis.

The redesign​

The principle was simple: Redis should store ordering and coordination data, not full delayed-job payloads.

So instead of treating Redis as the system of record for every postponed message, we introduced a storage abstraction with multiple backends. Redis stayed in the hot path, but it no longer carried the full job body in every case.

New storage strategy​

The storage decision depends on a few factors:

  • how long the delay will be
  • how large the payload is
  • whether a backend is enabled for that queue or tenant
  • whether the preferred backend is available

For short delays or small payloads, Redis can still hold the full object.

For longer delays or larger payloads, the job body goes somewhere cheaper, such as S3, and Redis stores only a compact reference.

A delayed-job member might look like this:

s3::some-queue::384192393192::<generated-uuid>

The prefix tells the processor which backend to use later. In this case, it fetches the payload through the s3 storage implementation before re-enqueueing the job.

Practical migration path​

You do not need to move everything at once.

  1. Start by adding the storage abstraction and keeping Redis as the default.
  2. Route one queue or one tenant to the new backend.
  3. Measure latency impact and failure modes.
  4. Expand gradually.

The key is making the reference format self-describing so the processor does not need external configuration to know where to look.

Supporting changes​

We also changed the delayed-job processing model itself. Each change supported the same principle: Redis coordinates, other systems store.

Decoupled from the scheduler​

The delayed-job service now drives itself on an interval instead of depending on another scheduler component. That removes an unnecessary coupling that could block draining.

Circuit breaking​

We added limits so a bad retry pattern cannot trap the processor forever in one hot loop. This prevents one queue from monopolizing the coordination layer.

Concurrent queue processing​

Instead of serializing too much work, we process multiple target queues concurrently with explicit limits. That improves draining speed while keeping resource usage controlled.

Better visibility​

We added metrics for:

  • re-enqueue latency
  • success and failure counts
  • number of delayed jobs processed
  • queue-level behavior over time

Delayed-job systems often fail quietly until they fail loudly. Visibility is part of making the coordination layer operable.

Horizontal scaling​

By changing how we pop work from Redis, we could run multiple delayed-job processors with a cleaner coordination model than before.

That made the system more resilient under abnormal load.

Expected impact​

The biggest expected win was infrastructure size.

If Redis stores compact references instead of large payloads for long-lived delayed jobs, memory usage drops dramatically. In our case, the difference was large enough to justify the redesign by itself.

The broader goals were:

  • much smaller Redis memory usage
  • faster draining under stress
  • less coupling between queues and backend components
  • better support for horizontal scaling
  • clearer operational visibility

Remaining concerns​

No redesign removes all trade-offs. The important questions after the change were:

  • how many delayed jobs per second can we sustain?
  • what do p75, p90, and p99 latencies look like?
  • how much will S3 fetch latency affect re-enqueue speed?
  • how low can we safely size Redis now?
  • what happens if Redis itself becomes unavailable?

Those are much healthier questions than "why is this one queue consuming hundreds of gigabytes of memory?"

Takeaway​

Redis is extremely useful for coordination and ordering, but that does not mean it should hold every byte of every object forever.

The best Redis designs often store less application data and more control-plane data.

In our case, moving from full payloads to references turned Redis from an expensive payload warehouse into a fast coordination layer.

When to consider this pattern​

Consider moving payloads out of Redis when:

  • your Redis memory is dominated by job bodies, not queue metadata
  • you have jobs delayed for minutes or hours with large payloads
  • your Redis instance size is driven by peak backlog, not steady-state load
  • you already have cheap object storage available
item.author: lucas.polesello@lwpsoftwares.com (Lucas Weis Polesello)

item.title: Redis is More Than a Cache - Delaying Jobs
item.description: How we used Redis sorted sets to delay and retry jobs, and why the consumption pattern matters more than the data structure.
item.content:encoded:
At Luma Health, our backend ran on RabbitMQ and async jobs. Delayed execution was not an edge case. It was a core feature.

A job might need to wait because an external system was down. Or it might just need a retry in five seconds. Either way, we needed a place to hold work until it was time to publish it again.

Redis seemed perfect. Our first design was simple. It also fell apart under load.

The lesson of this post is that the access pattern matters as much as the data structure choice.

The original design​

When a handler wanted to postpone a job, we serialized the job as JSON and stored it in a Redis sorted set. The score was the retry timestamp.

Every five seconds, a scheduled process would:

  1. read the set of delayed-job queues
  2. fetch ready jobs with ZRANGEBYSCORE
  3. publish them back to RabbitMQ
  4. remove them from the sorted set

It was easy to explain. It was also easy to break.

Where it failed​

Three problems kept getting worse:

  1. It could not catch up with large backlogs. We fetched jobs in chunks, so a fast-growing queue could stay behind indefinitely.

  2. It used too much memory. Large batches were loaded into memory before being removed.

  3. It was awkward to scale horizontally. The fetch-then-delete model made multi-consumer coordination harder than it needed to be.

At its worst, the system pushed Redis memory usage toward tens of gigabytes and still struggled to drain delayed work.

All three problems had the same root cause: we were reading work in large batches and deleting it separately.

How to know if you have the same problem​

Check whether your service:

  • fetches batches but sometimes fails to delete them atomically
  • grows in memory as the backlog grows
  • cannot safely run multiple instances
  • hits a throughput ceiling regardless of resources

If any of those sound familiar, the consumption pattern is likely the bottleneck, not Redis itself.

The change that mattered​

We kept the same Redis data structure and changed only how we consumed it.

Instead of fetching large batches into memory, we moved to a streaming-style approach:

  1. estimate ready jobs with ZCOUNT
  2. create an async iterator
  3. consume jobs one by one with ZPOPMIN
  4. publish each job as it is popped

The key change was switching from fetch-then-delete to pop-as-you-go.

ZPOPMIN worked well because it removes the lowest-score item as part of the read.

So instead of:

  • read a large batch
  • keep it in memory
  • publish it
  • delete it later

we now had:

  • pop one ready job
  • publish it
  • move on

Why this was better​

Lower memory pressure​

We stopped pulling large delayed-job batches into memory, so the process became much more stable.

Better backlog behavior​

Throughput was still limited by Redis and publish speed, but the processor no longer paid the same large batch cost up front.

Easier scaling​

Using destructive reads made coordination simpler than the previous model.

It did not solve every distributed-systems problem, but it gave us a much better base.

Results​

The results were better than the size of the code change suggested.

  • we drained a backlog that had grown to roughly 40 GB
  • process memory dropped from multi-gigabyte levels to a few hundred megabytes
  • Redis sizing pressure fell enough to affect infrastructure cost

This was one of those cases where the win came from using the same data structure in a better way.

The broader point​

This is why I like saying Redis is more than a cache.

A sorted set is not just a place to store temporary values. With the right access pattern, it can act as a lightweight time-ordered work queue.

The hard part is usually not picking Redis. It is picking a consumption pattern that still works once the backlog stops being small.

Takeaways​

  • Redis sorted sets are a practical building block for delayed jobs.
  • Consumption strategy matters as much as data structure choice.
  • Streaming-style consumption ages better than loading big batches into memory.
  • A small algorithmic change can remove both cost and reliability problems.

What to check in your own system​

If you already use Redis for delayed jobs:

  1. Are you fetching and then deleting, or removing as you read?
  2. Does your processor memory grow with backlog size?
  3. Can you safely run more than one consumer?
  4. Do you have visibility into queue depth, processing lag, and consumer health?

If the answer to any of those questions concerns you, the fix may be simpler than replacing Redis.

Final thought​

There are many ways to implement delayed jobs. RabbitMQ plugins, dedicated schedulers, and other persistence layers are all fair options.

But in day-to-day engineering, the best improvement is often the one that solves the current problem with the least operational drama.

In our case, that meant keeping Redis and fixing how we consumed it.
item.author: lucas.polesello@lwpsoftwares.com (Lucas Weis Polesello)

item.title: The Zalgo Effect and Resource Leakage
item.description: How mixing sync and async code in NodeJS caused RabbitMQ channel exhaustion in production, and why caching the promise instead of the result fixes it.
item.content:encoded:
In JavaScript, Zalgo usually refers to APIs that behave synchronously in some paths and asynchronously in others.

In practice, you see the same kind of bug when sync-looking control flow is mixed with async initialization and timing becomes part of correctness.

You may not notice it in a toy example, but you absolutely notice it in production.

This post is about one specific variant: lazy initialization that races with itself under concurrency, and the single change that fixes it.

The incident​

One day our SRE team started receiving PagerDuty alerts from services that were repeatedly restarting with errors like:

Error: No channels left to allocate

The affected services used RabbitMQ. Channel allocation was being exhausted, which meant something was creating channels and not reusing them properly.

The failing service mostly fanned out messages to many other services. At first glance, that should have been simple: create a queue abstraction, publish concurrently, move on.

But RabbitMQ management showed that the connection had accumulated far more channels than expected.

Something was leaking.

Why it only happened sometimes​

That was the clue.

If a resource leak appears only under certain timing conditions, the bug often lives in initialization or concurrency rather than in a simple missing cleanup call.

Our messaging abstraction dated back to the Node 4 era, when callback-style code was still the default. It had grown around that model for years.

The queue abstraction had two channel concepts:

  1. a consume channel, created during connection setup
  2. a confirm channel, created lazily when publishing

The problem lived in the second one.

The bug pattern​

The publish path roughly did this:

  1. check whether confirmChannel already exists
  2. if it does, return it
  3. if it does not, create it asynchronously and assign it later

That seems harmless until two concurrent publish operations hit the same check before the first initialization finishes.

In that window:

  • request A sees no channel and starts creating one
  • request B also sees no channel and starts creating one
  • both channels are created
  • the last assignment wins
  • the earlier channel stays open but is no longer referenced

That is the leak.

Not because RabbitMQ was strange, but because our lazy initialization was racing with itself.

How to spot this in your own code​

Look for patterns like this:

if (!this.resource) {
  this.resource = await createResource();
}

If that check runs concurrently, you have the same race condition. The fix is to cache the promise, not the result.

Why this is Zalgo-shaped​

The core problem was that resource existence was checked synchronously while resource creation finished asynchronously.

That made the correctness of the code depend on timing.

Whenever timing becomes part of the contract by accident, Zalgo is usually nearby.

The fix​

The first fix we shipped was conservative: initialize the channel once, await it, and only then fan out the publish operations.

That was the lowest-risk change.

The more robust long-term fix is a standard pattern:

  • store the initialization promise itself
  • return the same promise to all concurrent callers
  • replace or clear it if initialization fails and must be retried

In other words, do this:

class Queue {
  constructor(connection) {
    this.connection = connection;
    this.confirmChannelPromise = null;
  }

  getConfirmChannel() {
    if (!this.confirmChannelPromise) {
      this.confirmChannelPromise = this.connection.createConfirmChannel();
    }

    return this.confirmChannelPromise;
  }
}

Now the first caller starts creation, and every concurrent caller awaits the same promise.

No duplicate channels. No orphaned resources. No timing-dependent correctness.

Why this works​

The important detail is that assigning the promise is synchronous.

The async work still happens later, but the decision that initialization is already in progress happens immediately in the same synchronous execution block.

That gives you one shared future instead of several competing initializations.

The core lesson​

When lazily initializing async resources in NodeJS, do not cache only the resolved resource.

Cache the promise.

That one detail is the difference between:

  • one initialization
  • and several expensive, leaking, racing initializations

When this pattern does not apply​

Do not use promise caching when:

  • the resource must be recreated after every failure
  • initialization is intentionally retried with different parameters
  • you need to force re-initialization on every call for testing

In those cases, use explicit initialization or a factory instead.

Final thought​

A lot of old NodeJS systems carry abstractions built in a callback-first world and then gradually adapted to promises.

That is exactly where bugs like this tend to hide.

If a resource is created lazily and accessed concurrently, inspect the initialization path carefully. If the code checks synchronously and assigns asynchronously, you may already have a leak waiting for traffic.

Quick code-review checklist​

  • Lazy initialization happens in a concurrent path
  • The existence check is synchronous but the creation is async
  • The result is cached but the promise is not
  • There is no retry or failure cleanup logic
item.author: lucas.polesello@lwpsoftwares.com (Lucas Weis Polesello)

item.title: NodeJS 101 - Lazy Initialization
item.description: Two practical patterns for async resource initialization in NodeJS, and how to choose between them.
item.content:encoded:
Here is a small problem that never looks hard until it bites you.

You build a class that needs a database connection. The constructor runs, but the connection is not ready yet. Someone calls a method. It fails.

The core question is not how to connect. It is how to model the lifecycle so the caller knows when the resource is safe to use.

These are the two patterns I reach for. The post is about when each one fits, and why I prefer the first.

1. Explicit initialization​

The simplest option is to make initialization part of the API.

The caller creates the object, then calls connect() before using it.

const redis = require('redis');
const crypto = require('crypto');

class DistributedDataStructure {
  constructor() {
    this.client = redis.createClient();
  }

  async connect() {
    await this.client.connect();
  }

  async add(staffName, reviewId) {
    const accountName = await this.client.get(staffName);
    return this.client.sAdd(`v1:${accountName}:pending-reviews`, reviewId);
  }
}

(async () => {
  const ds = new DistributedDataStructure();
  await ds.connect();
  await ds.add('Jerome', crypto.randomBytes(12).toString('hex'));
})();

Why this works well​

  • easy to understand
  • initialization is explicit
  • failures happen in one place
  • very little hidden behavior

Trade-off​

The caller must remember to call connect().

In a small codebase, that may be fine. In a bigger or older one, someone will eventually forget.

How to spot the problem​

If you see classes that throw connection errors on first method call, or tests that mysteriously fail unless run in a certain order, check whether initialization is explicit.

2. Proxy-based lazy initialization​

Sometimes you need to create the object in synchronous code, but you still want the first real method call to wait for setup.

That is where a Proxy can help.

The example below is a pattern sketch, not a canonical Redis recipe. The exact readiness signal depends on the client library.

const redis = require('redis');
const { once } = require('events');
const crypto = require('crypto');

class ProxiedDistributedDataStructure {
  constructor() {
    this.client = redis.createClient();
    this.client.connect();

    return new Proxy(this, {
      get(target, property) {
        const value = target[property];
        if (typeof value !== 'function') {
          return value;
        }

        return async function (...args) {
          if (!target.client.isReady) {
            await once(target.client, 'ready');
          }
          return value.apply(target, args);
        };
      },
    });
  }

  async add(staffName, reviewId) {
    const accountName = await this.client.get(staffName);
    return this.client.sAdd(`v1:${accountName}:pending-reviews`, reviewId);
  }
}

(async () => {
  const ds = new ProxiedDistributedDataStructure();
  await ds.add('Jerome', crypto.randomBytes(12).toString('hex'));
})();

When this helps​

Use this when:

  • the object must be created in synchronous code
  • changing all callers is too expensive
  • you are working with legacy initialization flows
  • you want the first method call to absorb setup cost

Trade-offs​

This is nicer for callers, but harder to reason about.

A method call that looks immediate may now wait on hidden setup work.

That means:

  • debugging gets harder
  • errors can show up later
  • method wrapping needs care
  • the abstraction is easier to break

A common mistake​

If the initialization fails, the proxy will likely retry on every method call. Consider caching the promise itself and handling rejection explicitly, or adding a failed-state guard.

So while the Proxy approach is useful, I still treat it as the riskier tool.

The decision framework​

The broader point is that async resource initialization is part of your API design, and the choice between patterns depends on who controls the caller.

A simple heuristic:

  • use connect() when you own the callers and want a clear lifecycle
  • use a Proxy when you cannot change the callers and compatibility matters more
  • use a factory function when you can afford to make construction async

Final note​

Some client libraries already provide buffering or connection management that reduces the need for custom lazy wrappers.

Either way, make the initialization contract explicit, or hide it very carefully.

Quick checklist​

Before adding lazy initialization, ask:

  • Can I make the constructor caller async instead?
  • Will the first-method-call latency surprise anyone?
  • What happens if initialization fails?
  • Can I test this without mocking the Proxy behavior?
item.author: lucas.polesello@lwpsoftwares.com (Lucas Weis Polesello)
